coredata.dc:title: Green preparation of GQDs with small particle size, low defects, and high quantum yield by using hydrodynamic cavitation technology

coredata.dc:description: In this work, the hydrodynamic cavitation (HC) method with simple operation, low cost, high safety and large-scale production was applied to prepare GQDs. The expandable graphite powder as the raw material was treated by utilizing a microwave oven and then continuously exfoliated in venturi HC device. Finally, the GQDs with the advantages of small particle size, uniform size distribution, low defects, good water solubility and high quantum yield can be obtained by simple filtration. The GQDs are prepared by using the HC method without the use of acids and the introduction of impurities in the entire preparation process. The effects of HC cycle time, inlet pressure and fluid temperature on the property of GQDs were investigated. The results showed that GQDs with particle size of 1.77 ± 0.03 nm, size distribution in the range of 1.25–2.25 nm and fluorescence quantum yield of 36.77 % were obtained at 14 h HC cycling time, 3.0 bar inlet pressure and 50 °C temperature. The obtained GQDs solution has the advantages of strong fluorescence, high concentration and good stability. According to the atomic force microscope (AFM) image analysis, it can be seen that the HC technique can be used to prepare single-layer GQDs. It is hoped that the present work can provide a possibility for the preparation of few-layer GQDs on a certain scale.

head.ce:title: Green preparation of GQDs with small particle size, low defects, and high quantum yield by using hydrodynamic cavitation technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the hydrodynamic cavitation (HC) method with simple operation, low cost, high safety and large-scale production was applied to prepare GQDs. The expandable graphite powder as the raw material was treated by utilizing a microwave oven and then continuously exfoliated in venturi HC device. Finally, the GQDs with the advantages of small particle size, uniform size distribution, low defects, good water solubility and high quantum yield can be obtained by simple filtration. The GQDs are prepared by using the HC method without the use of acids and the introduction of impurities in the entire preparation process. The effects of HC cycle time, inlet pressure and fluid temperature on the property of GQDs were investigated. The results showed that GQDs with particle size of 1.77 ± 0.03 nm, size distribution in the range of 1.25–2.25 nm and fluorescence quantum yield of 36.77 % were obtained at 14 h HC cycling time, 3.0 bar inlet pressure and 50 °C temperature. The obtained GQDs solution has the advantages of strong fluorescence, high concentration and good stability. According to the atomic force microscope (AFM) image analysis, it can be seen that the HC technique can be used to prepare single-layer GQDs. It is hoped that the present work can provide a possibility for the preparation of few-layer GQDs on a certain scale.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Since graphene was first discovered in 2004 [1], it has attracted tremendous attention and interest of researchers due to its excellent properties (for example, high specific surface area, good conductivity and good chemical stability, etc.) [2]. However, with the continuous innovation and rapid development of science and technology, graphene with large particle size (micron-sized) has not completely met the future applications in biomedicine [3], sensors [4] and nanodevices [5]. Therefore, in order to obtain very small nano-sized graphene, the so-called graphene quantum dot (GQDs), some simple and practicable preparation methods were developed. The GQDs were defined as graphene with a particle size of <100 nm and a thickness of <10 layers [6]. GQDs are one of the latest superstars in the family of carbon nanoallotropic substances. Since the GQDs emerged in 2008, they have attracted a lot of attention due to their remarkable optical, electrical and photoelectric properties [7–9]. The optical and physical properties of GQDs differ from those of other members of the carbon material family, such as carbon nanotubes, fullerenes and graphene, mainly because of their significant quantum confinement and edge effects [10,11]. Compared to graphene and other carbon-based materials, GQDs were found to be able to accommodate more active sites (such as edges, functional groups and dopants) due to the particle's ultra-small size and very large specific surface area [12,13]. Due to the GQDs' excellent dispersion, highly adjustable chemophysical properties, chemical stability, low toxicity and biocompatibility, they have aroused great research enthusiasm in the fields of optics [14–17], biomedicine [18,19], photocatalysis [20] and solar cells [21].

There have been many reports on the preparation methods of GQDs [22–29]. So far, the preparation methods of GQDs are usually divided into two categories (namely, “bottom-up” and “top-down”). The “bottom-up” methods usually utilize stepwise chemical reactions of small molecule precursors (for example, citric acid [22], glucose [23], 1,3,6-trinitropyrene [24] and 4-carboxy-2,6-dimethylphenyl [25], etc.) to gain the desired GQDs. Dong et al. used citric acid as a small molecule precursor to get GQDs by pyrolyzing citric acid and the particle size of the obtained GQDs was around 15 nm [22]. In recent years, more and more “bottom-up” methods (for example, hydrothermal [26], microwave-assisted synthesis [27] and soft template methods [28], etc.) for the preparation of GQDs have been developed by combining some auxiliary techniques. With the continuous in-depth research of emerging methods, the vision of preparing GQDs with small particle size is gradually becoming a reality. Guo et al. used hydrothermal method to synthesize amine-functionalized GQDs through the use of 1,5-dinitronaphthalene as a precursor and the particle size of produced GQDs is in the range of 1.0–3.0 nm [30]. Although the particle size of prepared GQDs by using “bottom-up” methods is relatively small, these methods require complex and time-consuming purification operations. This is because these methods are difficult to ensure that all small molecule precursors are involved in the reaction and that they all produce the desired GQDs during the whole preparation process. In conclusion, the use of the “bottom-up” methods will bring high production costs and cause secondary pollution to the environment to a certain extent. Therefore, these methods are not conducive to the industrial production of GQDs.

The opposite of the “bottom-up” methods is the “top-down” methods. In contrast to the “bottom-up” methods, the “top-down” methods usually use bulk carbon materials (for example, graphite [31], reduced graphene oxide [32] and graphene oxide [33], etc.) as raw material. Under harsh experimental conditions, the strong oxidative ability of concentrated sulfuric and nitric acids is used to treat the raw material and then GQDs can be obtained after a long reaction time. Ye et al. used a variety of coal as raw material to prepare GQDs. Firstly, these coal were treated by using an ultrasound technique in concentrated sulfuric and nitric acids and then heated in an oil bath for 24 h. Finally, the solution pH was adjusted to 7.0 by adding sodium hydroxide and the particle size of obtained GQDs is around 3.0 nm [34]. Recently, with the further exploration of researchers, the “top-down” methods (for example, ultrasound [35,36], solvothermal [37], oxidation/reduction cutting [38] and electrochemical cutting, etc.) have also been expanded. Although the raw materials used in these “top-down” methods are cheaper and more readily available than those used in the “bottom-up” methods, the experimental conditions are more harsh and the size distribution of GQDs cannot be precisely controlled. Moreover, due to the complexity of operation, these above-mentioned “top-down” methods are also difficult to carry out large-scale industrial production in reality. Therefore, with the increasing demand for GQDs, there is an urgent need to develop a simple operation, cheap and readily acquired raw materials, controllable particle size and scaled production of GQDs.

Recently, the hydrodynamic cavitation (HC) as an advanced oxidation technique has been pursued by many researchers. Due to the advantages of inexpensive equipment, simple operation, low running cost and high processing capacity [39], the HC technique has been widely used in many fields (for example, disinfection [40], environmental remediation [41], biodiesel synthesis [42], biogas production [43] etc.). These applications primarily utilize the HC effect produced in HC device. When fluid flows into a throttling device (orifice plate [44], venturi tube [45] and nozzle [46], etc.), the flow velocity of the fluid will increase and the internal pressure of the fluid will decrease rapidly. When the internal pressure drops below the saturation vapor pressure of the solution, part of the fluid will be vaporized and the vaporized fluid will form cavities. With the further decrease of pressure, the cavities will gradually become larger, forming unstable cavitation bubbles with large amounts of energy [47]. When the fluid flows through the throttling device, the pressure starts to recover and the unstable cavitation bubbles will be compressed by constantly recovery pressure, which will eventually lead to the fracture and collapse. The fracture and collapse of cavitation bubbles can create high temperature and high pressure and release a large quantity of energy, accompanying by generation of violent shock waves, strong shear force and high speed jet flow [48]. Apparently, under the conditions of high temperature and high pressure, these violent shock waves, strong shear force and high speed jet flow can be used to exfoliate bulk carbon material to obtain GQDs. Moreover, the HC technology has been reported as a highly energy-efficient and suitable method for large-scale applications of biomass pretreatment [50]. Considering the above advantages, HC technology is considered to be able to prepare GQDs on a large scale.

In this study, the abundant and inexpensive expandable graphite powder was chosen as the starting raw material to prepare GQDs. First of all, the expandable graphite powder was treated by utilizing a household microwave oven to perform microwave expansion and then the GQDs were prepared in HC device. For the sample solution after HC treatment, only a simple filtration is needed to obtain the pure GQDs solution. The unreacted and insufficiently exfoliated graphite flakes can be recovered for recycling in theory. In addition, the GQDs obtained in this work are water-soluble and do not contain other impurities. Compared with the GQDs generated by adding organic solvents or strong acids, the properties of the GQDs prepared in this work will not be affected by the added substances during the preparation process and its luminescence is much purer and the quality of the finished product is higher. It is reported that the effects of pressure and fluid temperature on the performance of microfluidics were extremely important [51]. So, the effects of HC cycle time, inlet pressure and fluid temperature on the property of GQDs were investigated. Finally, it is hoped that this study can provide a possibility for the future low-cost and green preparation of GQDs on a certain scale.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Material
ce:section.ce:para: The expandable graphite powder (50 mesh, 99 %) was purchased from Zhengzhou Jingguo Graphite Co., Ltd., China. The self-priming pump was purchased from Japan Rijin pump industry Co., Ltd., Japan. The flowmeter was purchased from Yongjia Aocheng hardware Co., Ltd., China. The pressure gauges were purchased from Hongqi instrument Co., Ltd., China. The venturi tube was purchased from Donghai Quartz product Co., Ltd., China. The quartz cuvette with four optical paths (length × width × height: 42 mm × 12 mm × 12 mm) was purchased from China Yixing Weixin Instrument Co., LTD. The deionized water was used in the preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental setup
The pretreatment of the expandable graphite powder
ce:section.ce:para: Nine parts of 10 g expandable graphite powder were weighed and then each of them was placed in a household microwave oven (750 W) to perform microwave irradiation for 10 s. The purpose of the above operation is to carry out microwave expansion of the expandable graphite powder, which makes subsequent exfoliating operation easier to conduct. The expanded graphite powder was used for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of GQDs at different HC cycle time
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, three parts of 10 g expanded graphite powder were placed in three beakers containing 5.0 L deionized water, respectively. After stirring uniformly, the deionized water was continuously added into the beakers until the volume of sample suspension liquid reached 8.0 L. Then, the above-mentioned sample suspension liquid was poured into the holding tank and the HC cycle experiment was carried out at different HC cycle time (6.0 h, 10 h and 14 h). The filling degree of the holding tank was set to 50 % and the internal pressure within the liquid holding tank equates to atmospheric pressure (1.0 atm) because its interior is connected to the atmosphere. The switch of the self-priming pump is opened, the self-priming pump begins to work and then the suspension liquid enters into the HC device (venturi tube). The effect of the HC cycle time on the preparation of GQDs was explored under the experimental conditions (3.0 bar inlet pressure and 50 °C fluid temperature). The parameters of used venturi tube are 2.0 mm throat length, 22° semi-convergence angle, 6.0° semi-divergence angle and 2.0 mm diameter rounded throat. These three GQDs solutions (labeled GQDs (6.0), GQDs (10) and GQDs (14)) can be obtained by simple filtration using a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane after HC treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of GQDs at different venturi inlet pressure
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, three parts of 10 g expanded graphite powder were placed in three beakers containing 5.0 L deionized water, respectively. After stirring uniformly, the deionized water was continuously added into the beakers until the volume of sample suspension liquid reached 8.0 L. Then, the above-mentioned sample suspension liquid was poured into the holding tank and the HC cycle experiment was performed for 10 h HC cycle time under different venturi inlet pressures (2.0 bar, 3.0 bar, and 4.0 bar). The filling degree of the holding tank was set to 50 % and the internal pressure within the liquid holding tank equates to atmospheric pressure (1.0 atm) because its interior is connected to the atmosphere. The switch of the self-priming pump is opened, the self-priming pump begins to work and then the suspension liquid enters into the HC device (venturi tube). The effect of the venturi inlet pressure on the preparation of GQDs was explored under the experimental conditions (10 h HC cycle time and 50 °C fluid temperature). The parameters of used venturi tube are 2.0 mm throat length, 22° semi-convergence angle, 6.0° semi-divergence angle and 2.0 mm diameter rounded throat. These three GQDs solutions (labeled GQDs (2.0), GQDs (3.0) and GQDs (4.0)) can be obtained by simple filtration using a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane after 10 h HC cycle treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of GQDs at different fluid temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, three parts of 10 g expanded graphite powder were placed in three beakers containing 5.0 L deionized water, respectively. After stirring uniformly, the deionized water was continuously added into the beakers until the volume of sample suspension liquid reached 8.0 L. Then, the above-mentioned sample suspension liquid was poured into the holding tank and the HC cycle experiment was performed for 10 h HC cycle time under different fluid temperatures (40 °C, 50 °C and 60 °C). The filling degree of the holding tank was set to 50 % and the internal pressure within the liquid holding tank equates to atmospheric pressure (1.0 atm) because its interior is connected to the atmosphere. The switch of the self-priming pump is opened, the self-priming pump begins to work and then the suspension liquid enters into the HC device (venturi tube). The effect of the fluid temperatures on the preparation of GQDs was explored under the experimental conditions (10 h HC cycle time and 3.0 bar inlet pressure). The parameters of used venturi tube are 2.0 mm throat length, 22° semi-convergence angle, 6.0° semi-divergence angle and 2.0 mm diameter rounded throat. These three GQDs solutions (labeled GQDs (40), GQDs (50) and GQDs (60)) can be obtained by simple filtration using 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane after 10 h HC cycle treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The calculation of quantum yield of prepared GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
An important parameter, the relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY), can be used to characterize the fluorescence property of fluorescent material. In general, to determine the relative fluorescence QY of the fluorescent material, it is necessary to select a standard substance with known QY as reference, but this method is only applicable to liquid samples. The methods for the determination of relative fluorescence QY are usually divided into the one-point method and the slope method. In this work, the slope method is employed to determine the relative fluorescence QY of GQDs solution. The specific steps are as follows. Firstly, a series of different concentrations of GQDs solutions and standard substance solutions (Rhodamine B ethanol solution (QY = 82 %)) with absorbance values of <0.05 are tested by using the ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer. The intersection (at 252 nm) of the UV–vis absorption spectra of the GQDs solution and the standard substance solution is chosen as the excitation wavelength. In order to obtain integrated fluorescence intensity, the fluorescence spectrometer is used to gain the fluorescence curves of all above-mentioned GQDs solutions and standard substance solutions under the excitation wavelength of 252 nm. The integrated fluorescence intensity is the integrated area of the fluorescence curve in the wavelength range of 360–660 nm. The absorbance and corresponding integral fluorescence intensity values are measured for the above-mentioned GQDs solutions and standard substance solutions. Finally, the fluorescence QYs of the GQDs solutions are calculated according to formula (1). The relational formula among the fluorescence QY, fluorescence intensity and absorbance of GQDs solution and standard substance solution is as follows [55].
(1)

φ S

F S
where φ is the fluorescence QY, η is the refractive index of the solvent, F is the integrated area of the fluorescence spectrum of the solutions, A is the absorbance of the solutions, X represents the GQDs solutions and S represents the standard substance solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The calculation of cavitation number
ce:section.ce:para:
In hydromechanics, a dimensionless parameter, the cavitation number (C v), can be used to estimate the strength of the HC effect produced by HC device. Generally, when the HC effect occurs, the C v value is <1.0. Specifically, when the C v value is between 0.1 and 1.0, a smaller C v value represents a stronger HC effect. However, when the C v value is <0.1, the further decrease of the C v value does not mean that the HC effect becomes stronger, which is due to the fact that the supercavitation phenomenon is generated at this time. The occurrence of supercavitation phenomenon will lead to poor HC effect. Therefore, the C v is essential for determining the strength of the HC effect. The C v value can be calculated by the following two formulas [41].
(2)
C V

P 2
