where C
v is the cavitation number, P 1 is the upstream inlet pressure of the HC device, P 2 is the downstream recovery pressure of the HC device, P v is the saturated vapor pressure of the fluid at the solution temperature, V is the fluid flow velocity and ρ is the density of the fluid at the solution temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structure of the prepared samples was determined by using the X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D-8, Bruker-axs, Germany). The morphology characteristic of the prepared samples was observed by using the scan electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6360LV, Hitachi Corporation, Japan). The particle size, size distribution and crystal morphology of the prepared samples were explored by using the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Corporation, US). The elemental composition of the prepared samples was tested by using the X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi, Thermo Fisher Corporation, China). The optical properties of the prepared samples were analyzed by using the ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Hitachi, U-3310, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (FL, Cary Eclipse 3000, USA). The thickness of the prepared samples was measured by using the atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension Edge, Germany). The graphitization degree of the prepared samples was evaluated by using the Raman spectral analyzer (Raman, Thermo Scientific DXR, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
The morphological characterization of the samples
ce:section.ce:para: It is reported that the expandable graphite is stacked by millions of graphene layers [57]. Therefore, some methods (for example, the strong oxidizing power of concentrated sulfuric and nitric acids at high temperature, the strong penetrating power of supercritical fluid and the microwave expansion power of microwave technology, etc.) can be used to compulsively separate these graphene layers. For example, Ye et al. used anthracite, bituminous coal and coke as raw materials and utilized the strong oxidizing ability of concentrated sulfuric and nitric acids at high temperature to coercively separate these graphene layers [33]. Gao et al. used natural graphite as the raw material and utilized the strong penetrating power of ultrasound and supercritical carbon dioxide (scCO2) to compulsively open these stacked graphene layers [48]. In this work, the expandable graphite powder was treated by using the microwave technique. The microwave irradiation can weaken/disrupt the van der Waals interlayer bonds among different graphene layers, which leads to expansion of the expandable graphite powder. In order to observe the microwave expansion phenomenon of expandable graphite powder more clearly, the scan electron microscopy (SEM) technique was used to analyze the morphological characteristics of the prepared samples. Fig. 3(a) and (b) show the SEM images of expandable graphite powder before and after microwave irradiation, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the expandable graphite powder without microwave expansion is stacked by many graphene layers. The volume of the expandable graphite powder is relatively small and the combination among graphene layers is very tight. At this time, the expandable graphite powder exhibits high stability and presents significant difficult for direct exfoliation. However, in Fig. 3(b), the volume of the expanded graphite powder increases significantly, the layer-to-layer connection of graphene is disrupted and significant separation among the graphene layers occurs. At this time, the combination of graphene layers is very loose, which greatly reduces the difficulty of exfoliating expandable graphite powder. It is very conducive to the subsequent exfoliating operation. Moreover, the above-mentioned phenomenon indicates that the expandable graphite powder has been successfully expanded and also indicates that it is practicable to expand the expandable graphite powder by using microwave technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The elemental composition analysis of the prepared sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to analyze the elemental composition and functional group of the prepared GQDs samples, the GQDs (10) prepared under the conditions of 10 h HC cycle time, 3.0 bar venturi inlet pressure and 50 °C fluid temperature is used as the test sample and the X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was used to obtain the XPS spectra of the GQDs (10) and the related information is shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4(a–c) shows the full XPS spectrum of GQDs (10), the XPS fine spectrum of C 1s and the XPS fine spectrum of O 1s, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the full spectrum of GQDs (10) has two peaks at 285 eV and 532 eV, corresponding to C 1s and O 1s signals, respectively, which indicates that only the elements C and O are present in the full spectrum of GQDs (10) and the atomic percentages are 96.94 % and 3.06 %, respectively. This indicates that no impurity is introduced in the preparation process of GQDs by HC technique and the purity of the obtained GQDs is high. The deconvolution of high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 4(b)) shows that the C 1s peak consists of CC at 284.8 eV and CO at 285.8 eV. From Fig. 4(b), the content of CC is quite high, which indicates that there are few defects in the GQDs prepared using HC technique. The deconvolution of high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 4(c)) shows that the O 1s peak consists of CO at 530.54 eV, CO at 532.04 eV and surface-adsorbed water at 533.49 eV. The above-mentioned phenomenon is consistent with previous reported literature [58]. Based on the above analysis, it can be seen that no impurity elements are introduced into the prepared GQDs-10 sample and the sample has few defects. Furthermore, it also proves that the GQDs prepared by the HC technique are very pure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The thickness and graphitization degree analyses of the prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para: Raman spectroscopy was used to determine the graphitization degree of the GQDs. Fig. 5(a) shows the Raman spectra of expanded graphite powder and GQDs (prepared under the conditions of 3.0 bar venturi inlet pressure, 50 °C fluid temperature and 10 h HC cycle time). As can be seen from the figure, the Raman spectra of the expanded graphite powder and the GQDs both have two peaks, namely the D band at 1349 cm−1 and the G band at 1580 cm−1, respectively. This is consistent with previous reported literature [52]. The D band represents the degrees of defects and disorder of the carbon material and the G band reflects the degrees of lattice vibration and order of the carbon material. It can be found that the peaks of the GQDs in the D band are particularly low, which further indicates that the GQDs prepared by using the HC technique have low defects. In general, a small ratio of ID/IG indicates a high degree of graphitization and crystallinity of the sample. The ID/IG values of the expanded graphite powders and GQDs are 0.095 and 0.078, respectively. This suggests that GQDs prepared by HC technique have high graphitization degree and good crystallinity. In addition, the thickness of GQDs is analyzed by using atomic force microscopy (AFM). Fig. 5b shows the AFM image of GQDs prepared under the conditions of 3.0 bar venturi inlet pressure, 50 °C fluid temperature and 10 h HC cycle time. Fig. 5c and d show the corresponding height analysis of labeled GQDs. As can be seen from the figures, the thickness of GQDs is mainly around 1.5 nm, which corresponds to the thickness of 3–4 layers of graphene. In addition, the thickness of some GQDs is 0.318 nm, which corresponds to that of a single-layer graphene. This suggests that multi-layer or even single-layer GQDs can be prepared by using HC technique. Moreover, it can also be seen from the Fig. 5b that there are some GQDs with high-thickness. These high-thickness GQDs are surrounded by some low-thickness GQDs. This indicates that these high-thickness GQDs may be formed through the agglomeration of some low-thickness GQDs. This phenomenon may be caused by the absence of dispersant added during the preparation of GQDs or the influence of the AFM test method. In conclusion, it can be seen that GQDs with low defects and few layers can be prepared by using HC technique.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of HC cycle time on the composition, particle size, optical properties and stability of GQDs
Effect of HC cycle time on the composition and particle size of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was used to understand the effect of different HC cycle time (6.0 h, 10 h, and 14 h) on the crystal structure of GQDs obtained at venturi inlet pressure of 3.0 bar and fluid temperature of 50 °C. Fig. 6(a) shows the XRD patterns of GQDs prepared at different HC cycle time and the partially enlarged XRD pattern of GQDs gained at 10 h HC cycle time. Fig. 6(b) shows the related XRD standard card of carbon. From the figure, it can be clearly seen that the diffraction peak positions of the GQDs samples obtained at different HC cycle time are consistent with those of the standard diffraction pattern of graphite. The XRD patterns of the GQDs samples all include four different peaks. The peaks at 2θ values of 26.56°, 42.36°, 44.57°, and 54.69° correspond to the (002), (010), (011), and (004) crystal planes of the graphite, respectively. In addition, it can also be seen from the figure that the height of the diffraction peaks of the prepared GQDs gradually becomes low and that the width ((0.2707 (6.0 h), 0.3021 (10 h) and 0.3239 (14 h)) of the diffraction peaks gradually becomes wide with the extension of the HC cycle time. This phenomenon may be caused by the decrease of obtained GQDs particle size with the extension of the HC cycle time. Furthermore, this phenomenon can also be explained using the data calculated by Scherrer's formula (4) [49].
(4)
D =
αλ
β cos
θ
where, D is the particle size of the QDs, α is a constant of 0.94, λ is the wavelength of the X-rays (λ = 0.15406 for the Cu kα target), β is the full-width half-maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak and θ is the diffraction angle. It can be seen that the particle size is inversely proportional to the FWHM of the diffraction peak. Therefore, it can be hypothesized that the width of the diffraction peak gradually becomes wide with the extension of the HC cycle time, and that the particle size of the QDs instead becomes small. Ulteriorly, it can be considered that the particle size of the GQDs becomes smaller and smaller as the HC cycle time continues to increase. So, properly prolonging the HC cycle time is advantageous for the preparation of GQDs with small particle size.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) were utilized to more clearly and accurately observe the particle size, size distribution and crystal morphology of the GQDs prepared at different HC cycle times (6.0 h, 10 h and 14 h). Fig. 7(a–c) shows the TEM images of the GQDs obtained at different HC cycle time and the illustrations in the upper right corner of these three TEM images are the particle size distribution maps measured using Nano Measure 1.2 software. According to the method reported in the relevant literature, 100 GQDs particles were selected for particle size analysis to calculate the average particle size of GQDs [56]. In addition, the size range of the particle size distribution maps was chosen because most of the 100 GQDs particles selected were within this range. Fig. 7(d) shows the HR-TEM image of GQDs (10) gained under the conditions of 10 h HC cycle time, 3.0 bar venturi inlet pressure and 50 °C fluid temperature. It is obvious in Fig. 7(a–c) that the GQDs obtained at different HC cycle time (6.0 h, 10 h and 14 h) have spherical morphology and good dispersity. And with the extension of the HC cycle time, the particle size of GQDs becomes small and the size distribution range becomes narrow. Specifically, it can be seen from the size distribution maps that the average sizes of the GQDs produced at different HC cycle time are 2.28 ± 0.03 nm (6.0 h), 1.95 ± 0.03 nm (10 h) and 1.77 ± 0.03 nm (14 h), respectively, and the size distribution ranges of the attained GQDs are 1.25–3.75 nm (6.0 h), 1.0–3.0 nm (10 h) and 1.25–2.37 nm (14 h), respectively. It can be seen that the GQDs obtained at 14 h HC cycle time have the smallest particle size and the narrowest size distribution range. Compared with other reported studies, it can be seen that the particle size of GQDs prepared by HC technology is smaller and the size distribution is more uniform [52–54]. The above phenomenon may be due to the fact that the more times the expanded graphite powder is treated by the venturi HC device with the extension of the HC cycle time, the more thoroughly the expanded graphite powder is exfoliated, which will lead to smaller particle size and narrower size distribution of the obtained GQDs. In addition, the above-mentioned phenomenon is consistent with that observed using XRD pattern and both show that the particle size of GQDs becomes small as the HC cycle time increases. Furthermore, the lattice fringe of the GQDs prepared at the 10 h HC cycle time is analyzed. As can be seen from Fig. 7(d), the lattice spacing of the GQDs (10) is 0.203 nm, corresponding to the (011) crystal plane of graphite, which is consistent with the result of the previous XRD characterization. In conclusion, prolonging the HC cycle time is advantageous for the preparation of GQDs with small particle size. Moreover, the desired particle size of GQDs can be customized by adjusting the HC cycle time, which can meet the needs of different fields for GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of HC cycle time on optical properties of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrometer can be utilized to analyze the optical properties of the GQDs prepared at different HC cycle time (6.0 h, 10 h and 14 h). Specifically, the concentration changes of the samples are analyzed using UV–vis spectroscopy. This is because the UV–vis spectrum can be used to observe the change of the absorbance of the sample, and the absorbance can represent the amount of the QDs to a certain extent. For quantitative solvents, the magnitude of QDs number can indicate that of the concentration of the sample. Fig. 8 shows the UV–vis spectra of GQDs solutions prepared at different HC cycle time (6.0 h, 10 h and 14 h). The inset in the upper right corner shows a photograph of the three GQDs solutions under the irradiation of natural light. As can be seen from the inset, the obtained GQDs solutions have good water solubility and present a pale yellow color under the irradiation of natural light. However, they all show a blue luminescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 9(d)), which is consistent with previous reported literature [58]. In the figure, it can be found that there are two obvious absorption peaks (at 230 nm and 275 nm) for all GQDs solutions. Specifically, the typical absorption peak at 230 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition in the aromatic sp2 structural domain and the absorption peak at 275 nm may be ascribed to the n-π* transition between the oxygen-containing group and the sp2 structural domain [57]. In addition, it can be seen from the figure that the absorption peak intensities of the GQDs solution at 230 nm and 275 nm both gradually become stronger with the extension of HC cycle time, indicating that the concentration of the GQDs solution becomes higher and higher. This is also demonstrated by concentrations of GQDs solutions measured using the ICP technique. The concentrations of GQDs solutions are 0.88 g/L (6.0 h), 1.18 g/L (10 h) and 1.86 g/L (14 h), respectively. Further, it can be seen that the absorption peak width of GQDs solution gradually becomes narrow with the extension of HC cycle time, suggesting that the particle size of GQDs becomes more and more uniform. Theoretically, with the extension of HC cycle time, the more times the expanded graphite powder is treated by the venturi HC device, the more thoroughly the expanded graphite powder will be exfoliated and the GQDs with small particle size will become more and more abundant, resulting in a higher concentration of GQDs solution and a more uniform particle size distribution with the extension of HC cycle time. This conclusion is consistent with that obtained from the TEM image analysis. In conclusion, high concentration and uniform size distribution of GQDs can be obtained by prolonging the HC cycle time.

In order to further explore the optical properties of GQDs prepared at different HC cycle time (6.0 h, 10 h and 14 h), detailed PL spectra studies were carried out using different excitation wavelengths. The quartz cuvette with four optical paths was utilized in fluorescence measurements. Fig. 9(a–c) shows the PL spectra of the GQDs obtained at 6.0 h, 10 h and 14 h HC cycle time for different excitation wavelengths, respectively. Fig. 9(d) shows the emission spectra of the GQDs prepared at different HC cycle time at 225 nm excitation wavelength and the inset in the upper right corner of the figure is a photograph of the GQDs solutions obtained at different HC cycle time under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp. The effect of different excitation wavelengths on the emission spectra of GQDs is investigated in the excitation wavelength range of 205–285 nm. As can be seen from Fig. 9(a–c), when the excitation wavelength is lower than 225 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs prepared using HC technique gradually becomes large with the increase of the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum value at the 225 nm excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength is longer than 225 nm, the fluorescence intensity instead becomes small with the increase of the excitation wavelength. However, it can be clearly seen that the peak position of the emission spectra shows no significant red-shift or blue-shift at different excitation wavelengths. This phenomenon belongs to the typical excitation-independent PL behavior, which may be attributed to the homogeneous microstructure and similar chemical state of the GQDs prepared using the HC technique. For example, Li et al. prepared a low defect GQDs, whose PL spectra also showed the typical excitation-independent PL behavior [58]. Perhaps, this phenomenon that shows no significant red-shift or blue-shift may be caused by the low defect of the prepared GQDs. From the above XPS spectra analysis, it can be found that the GQDs prepared by the HC technique indeed have low defect.

In addition, the PL spectra also show that the strongest emission of the prepared GQDs occurs at 225 nm with 138 nm Stokes shift. Apparently, compared to the GQDs obtained using other preparation methods, the GQDs prepared by the HC technique demonstrate a larger Stokes shift [59]. The larger Stokes shift has the advantages of low background interference, good biocompatibility and high detection sensitivity. It can avoid the overlap of emission and excitation spectra, which is beneficial to the detection of fluorescence spectral signal. Fig. 9(d) shows that the fluorescence intensity of the prepared GQDs gradually increases with the extension of the HC cycle time at the 225 nm excitation wavelength. This is due to the increase of GQDs solution concentration and the decrease of GQDs particle size with the extension of HC cycle time. It can also be seen from the upper right inset that the luminescence of the obtained GQDs solution under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light is getting brighter and brighter with the extension of the HC cycle time.

Further, the fluorescence quantum yield of the GQDs solution is calculated to study the fluorescence properties of the GQDs solution. In this work, the calculated QYs are 18.82 % (6.0 h), 24.24 % (10 h) and 36.77 % (14 h), respectively. It can be seen that the fluorescence QY of the obtained GQDs solution is getting larger and larger with the increase of the HC cycle time, which may be related to the concentration of the GQDs solution and the particle size of the GQDs. Moreover, the fluorescence QY of the GQDs solution prepared using the HC technique is higher than previous those reported by researchers (the relevant data are exhibited in Table 1 ), which may be related to the preparation method of GQDs. GQDs prepared using HC technique are pure because no additives are used in the preparation process. Although the use of additives may increase the concentration of the QDs and reduce the difficulty of the exfoliation operation, these additives may affect the optical properties of QDs. In summary, it can be seen that the optical properties of GQDs prepared using the HC technique are excellent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of HC cycle time on the stability of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis spectra of the GQDs samples were periodically tested to explore the stability of the GQDs solution prepared at different HC cycle time (6.0 h, 10 h and 14 h). Specifically, the peak value of the first exciton excitation peak in the UV–vis spectrum of the GQDs sample tested for the first time is regarded as 100 %. Next, the percentages between the tested peak values obtained from the subsequent five weeks and the first week are used as vertical coordinate, and time is used as the horizontal coordinate to plot the stability curves. Fig. 10 shows the stability curves of the GQDs sample solutions obtained at different HC cycle time. It can be seen from the figure that the stability of GQDs (GQDs (10)) solution is the best and that of GQDs (GQDs (6.0)) solution is the worst in the initial three weeks. The stability of GQDs (GQDs (14)) solution is between those of GQDs (10) and GQDs (6.0) solutions. The above phenomenon may be attributed to the following factors. From the analysis of the TEM images in Fig. 7, it can be seen that the concentration of the prepared GQDs solution gradually becomes large and the particle size gradually becomes small with the extension of the HC cycle time. Due to the highest concentration of GQDs (14) sample solution and the biggest probability of collision among particles, the agglomeration phenomenon for GQDs (14) may occur easily at this time. The particle collisions of the GQDs (14) solution will be more intense and the agglomeration will occur more obviously compared to that of the GQDs (10) solution. Therefore, the stability of the GQDs (10) solution is higher than that of the GQDs (14) solution in the initial three weeks. The stability of GQDs (6.0) solution is the worst. This may be ascribed to the large particle size of GQDs (6.0) and the big attraction among particles, which causes particles to quickly attract each other to form much larger particles and then fall rapidly.

After three weeks, it can be seen that the stability of the GQDs (14) solution is the best and that of the GQDs (6.0) solution is the worst. The stability of the GQDs (10) solution is between those of the GQDs (14) and the GQDs (6.0) solutions. This phenomenon may be due to the fact that the particle size of the GQDs (10) solution after three weeks has agglomerated to a certain size and the sedimentation phenomenon for the GQDs (10) solution may occur at this time. Although the agglomeration of the GQDs (14) solution is initially intense, it can last for a long time due to the smallest started particle size and narrowest size distribution. The GQDs (14) solution after three weeks may still be in a state of agglomeration. Therefore, the stability of the GQDs (14) solution after three weeks is higher than that of the GQDs (10) solution. Since the GQDs (6.0) solution has been in a sedimentation state, the particle size will get bigger and bigger and their sedimentation speed will get faster and faster, which results in the poor solution dispersity and occurrence of precipitation. Therefore, the stability of the GQDs (6.0) solution is the worst after three weeks. Further, it can also be seen from the figure that the GQDs (14), GQDs (10) and GQDs (6.0) solutions show 5.65 %, 7.73 % and 16.03 % absorbance decrease, respectively, after 5 weeks. In summary, the overall stability of the GQDs (14) solution is the best and that of the GQDs (6.0) solution is the worst. The stability of the GQDs (10) solution is between those of the GQDs (14) and the GQDs (6.0) solutions. This indicates that the long HC cycle time can bring good stability and dispersity of the GQDs solution prepared by HC technique. Though no stabilizer is used in the preparation of the GQDs solution using HC technique, the stability of the obtained GQDs solution is good. In addition, the stability of the GQDs solution prepared by HC technique may decrease after prolonged storage. At this time, if the GQDs solution can be placed in the HC equipment for a short period of HC cycling operation, the good dispersion of the GQDs solution can be recovered quickly. This GQDs recovery operation is distinctive to HC technique. This operation allows the prepared GQDs solution to be in the best state at all times, which is very beneficial for the preparation and commercialization of GQDs on a certain scale.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of venturi inlet pressure on the composition, particle size, optical properties and stability of GQDs
Effect of venturi inlet pressure on the composition and particle size of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the inlet pressure of the cavitation device as an important parameter, has a decisive influence on the HC effect. Therefore, the influence of inlet pressure on the HC effect was investigated in the adjustable inlet pressure range [63]. The XRD technique was used to investigate the effect of different venturi inlet pressures (2.0 bar, 3.0 bar, and 4.0 bar) on the crystal structure of the GQDs prepared at 10 h HC cycle time and 50 °C fluid temperature. Fig. S1 shows the XRD patterns of GQDs prepared at different venturi inlet pressures and a standard PDF card (PDF#26-1076) of carbon. From the figure, it can be clearly seen that the XRD patterns of the GQDs samples all contain four diffraction peaks. The peaks at 2θ values of 26.56°, 42.36°, 44.57°, and 54.69° correspond to the (002), (010), (011), and (004) crystal planes of the graphite, respectively. This indicates that the diffraction peak positions of the GQDs samples obtained at different venturi inlet pressures are consistent with those of the standard diffraction pattern of graphite. Further, it can also be seen from the figure that the order of the diffraction peak heights of the obtained GQDs samples is 2.0 bar > 3.0 bar > 4.0 bar and the order of the diffraction peak widths (0.2607 (2.0 bar), 0.3021 (3.0 bar) and 0.3127 (4.0 bar)) is 2.0 bar < 3.0 bar < 4.0 bar at different venturi inlet pressures. This may be due to the fact that the particle size of the obtained GQDs decreases with the increase of venturi inlet pressure. It can be found that the diffraction peak height of the GQDs prepared at 4.0 bar venturi inlet pressure is the lowest and the diffraction peak width is the widest. Since the particle size of the QDs is inversely proportional to the half-peak width of the diffraction peak, the particle size of the GQDs obtained at 4.0 bar venturi inlet pressure is the smallest. In addition, the cavitation number C v can be used to further explain this phenomenon. The C v value can be used to represent the strength of the HC effect to some extent. When the C v value is between 0.1 and 1.0, a small C v value represents a strong HC effect. Specifically, according to formula (2), the C v values of GQDs obtained at different Venturi inlet pressures are 0.642 (2.0 bar), 0.448 (3.0 bar) and 0.298 (4.0 bar), respectively. Therefore, it can be seen that the C v value is the smallest and the produced HC effect is the strongest at 4.0 bar venturi inlet pressure, which will lead to the smallest particle size of the obtained GQDs. In conclusion, enhancing the venturi inlet pressure is beneficial for preparing GQDs with small particle size.

The TEM technology was utilized to more clearly and accurately observe the particle size and size distribution of the GQDs prepared at different venturi inlet pressures (2.0 bar, 3.0 bar and 4.0 bar). Fig. S2(a-c) shows the TEM images of the GQDs obtained at different venturi inlet pressures and the insets in the upper right corner of these three TEM images are the particle size distribution maps (the number of GQDs used for particle size analysis is 100). Some GQDs particles are marked with yellow circles in the figure. It is evident in Fig. S2(a–c) that the GQDs obtained at different venturi inlet pressures (2.0 bar, 3.0 bar and 4.0 bar) all have spherical morphology and good dispersity. Specifically, it can be seen from the size distribution maps that the average sizes of the GQDs produced at different venturi inlet pressures are 2.28 ± 0.03 nm (2.0 bar), 1.95 ± 0.03 nm (3.0 bar) and 1.90 ± 0.01 nm (4.0 bar), respectively, and the size distribution ranges of the attained GQDs are 1.25–3.5 nm (2.0 bar), 1.0–3.0 nm (3.0 bar) and 1.5–3.0 nm (4.0 bar), respectively. It can be found that the GQDs obtained at 2.0 bar venturi inlet pressure have the largest particle size and the widest size distribution and the GQDs obtained at 4.0 bar venturi inlet pressure have the smallest particle size and the narrowest size distribution. Compared with other reported studies, it can be seen that the particle size of GQDs prepared by HC technology is smaller and the size distribution is more uniform [52–54]. The above phenomenon may be due to the fact that the flow velocity through the venturi HC device increases, and the times of HC cycles become more (that is, the more times the expanded graphite powder is treated by the venturi HC device) when the venturi inlet pressure increases, which leads to smaller particle size and narrower size distribution of the obtained GQDs. In addition, the above-mentioned phenomenon is consistent with that observed using the XRD pattern and both show that the particle size of GQDs becomes small when the venturi inlet pressure increases. However, it can also be observed from the size distribution maps that the particle size of the GQDs obtained at 4.0 bar venturi inlet pressure only slightly decreased compared to the GQDs obtained at 3.0 bar venturi inlet pressure. Although the further increase of venturi inlet pressure is beneficial for the preparation of GQDs with small particle size, the particle size reduction magnitude of the obtained GQDs is small. Considering the safety of operation, the operating cost of the self-priming pump and the adverse effects (for example, the service life of the pump decreases under constant high pressure and the entire HC equipment will slightly shake due to the excessive of fluid flow velocity and the pump output power, which is not conducive to the stable operation of the HC equipment) of the HC equipment at high pressure during the preparation process of GQDs, 3.0 bar venturi inlet pressure is used in the actual production process to carry out the GQDs preparation on a certain scale.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of venturi inlet pressure on the optical properties of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to analyze the optical properties of the GQDs prepared at different venturi inlet pressures (2.0 bar, 3.0 bar and 4.0 bar). Fig. S3 shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of GQDs solutions prepared at different venturi inlet pressures. In the figure, it can be found that there are two obvious absorption peaks (at 230 nm and 275 nm) for all GQDs solutions. Specifically, the typical absorption peak at 230 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 structural domain and the absorption peak at 275 nm may be ascribed to the n-π* transition between the oxygen-containing group and the sp2 structural domain [57]. In addition, the UV–vis spectra can be used to analyze concentration changes of the samples. It can be seen that the peak intensity orders of the absorption peaks at 230 nm and 275 nm wavelengths of the GQDs solutions obtained at different venturi inlet pressures all are 2.0 bar < 3.0 bar < 4.0 bar. It can be found that the lowest/highest concentration of GQDs solution is obtained at the 2.0 bar/4.0 bar venturi inlet pressure, respectively, which suggests that increasing the venturi inlet pressure is beneficial for the preparation of high concentration of GQDs. The concentrations of GQDs solutions are determined by ICP technique and are 0.97 g/L (2.0 bar), 1.18 g/L (3.0 bar) and 1.76 g/L (4.0 bar), respectively. Further, it can also be seen that the width orders of the absorption peaks all are 2.0 bar > 3.0 bar > 4.0 bar. The width variation of the absorption peaks may be related to the size distribution of the prepared GQDs. It can be found that the absorption peak width of GQDs obtained at 2.0 bar venturi inlet pressure is the largest and that of GQDs obtained at 4.0 bar venturi inlet pressure is the smallest, which indicates that increasing the venturi inlet pressure is advantageous for the preparation of size-uniform GQDs. Theoretically, the flow velocity through the venturi HC device increases and the times of HC cycles become more (that is, the more times the expanded graphite powder is treated by the venturi HC device). Therefore, the GQDs with small particle size become more, the concentration of GQDs solution becomes larger and the particle size distribution becomes more uniform when the venturi inlet pressure increases. In addition, it can be noticed that the concentration and absorbance of the GQDs solution obtained at 4.0 bar venturi inlet pressure show a slight increase compared to those of GQDs obtained at 3.0 bar venturi inlet pressure. Although further increasing the venturi inlet pressure is favorable for preparing high concentrations of GQDs on the basis of 3.0 bar venturi inlet pressure, the concentration increase magnitude of the obtained GQDs is small. This is consistent with the conclusions obtained from TEM image analysis. In conclusion, high concentration and uniform size distribution of GQDs can be achieved by appropriately increasing the venturi inlet pressure.

In order to further explore the optical properties of GQDs prepared at different venturi inlet pressures (2.0 bar, 3.0 bar and 4.0 bar), detailed PL spectra studies were carried out using different excitation wavelengths. The quartz cuvette with four optical paths was utilized in fluorescence measurements. Fig. S4(a–c) shows the PL spectra of the GQDs obtained at 2.0 bar, 3.0 bar and 4.0 bar venturi inlet pressures under different excitation wavelengths, respectively. Fig. S4(d) shows the emission spectra of the GQDs solutions prepared at different venturi inlet pressures at 225 nm excitation wavelength and the inset in the upper right corner is a photograph of the GQDs solution under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp. The effect of different excitation wavelengths on the emission spectra of GQDs is investigated in the excitation wavelength range of 205–285 nm. As can be seen from Fig. S4(a–c), when the excitation wavelength is lower than 225 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs prepared using HC technique gradually increases with the increase of the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity reaches the highest value at the 225 nm excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength is longer than 225 nm, the fluorescence intensity instead decreases with the increase of the excitation wavelength. However, it can be clearly seen that the peak position of the emission spectra shows no significant red-shift or blue-shift phenomenon at different excitation wavelengths. This phenomenon belongs to the typical excitation-independent PL behavior, which may be attributed to the homogeneous microstructure, low defect and similar chemical state of the GQDs prepared using the HC technique.

Fig. S4(d) shows that the fluorescence intensity of the prepared GQDs becomes large when the venturi inlet pressure increases at the 225 nm excitation wavelength, which may be attributed to the increase of GQDs solution concentration and the decrease of GQDs particle size when the venturi inlet pressure increases. It can also be seen from the upper right inset that the luminescence of the obtained GQDs solution under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light gets brighter when the venturi inlet pressure increases. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yield of the GQDs solution is calculated to investigate the fluorescence properties of the GQDs solution. The calculated QYs are 19.28 % (2.0 bar), 24.24 % (3.0 bar) and 34.01 % (4.0 bar), respectively. It can be seen that the fluorescence QY of the obtained GQDs solution increases when the inlet pressure of the venturi increases, which may be related to the concentration of the GQDs solution and the particle size. In summary, it can be found that appropriately elevating the venturi inlet pressure is conducive to the preparation of GQDs with excellent optical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Venturi inlet pressure on the stability of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis spectra of the GQDs samples were periodically tested to evaluate the stability of the GQDs solution prepared by using different venturi inlet pressures (2.0 bar, 3.0 bar and 4.0 bar). Fig. S5 shows the percentage decrease in absorbance of GQDs solutions obtained at different venturi inlet pressures. From the figure, it can be observed that the decreased amount in absorbance of the GQDs (GQDs (4.0)) solution is smallest and that of GQDs (GQDs (2.0)) solution is the biggest. The decreased amount in absorbance of GQDs (GQDs (3.0)) solution is between those of GQDs (2.0) and GQDs (4.0) solutions. This may be related to the particle size and size distribution of GQDs prepared by using HC technique. From the analysis of the TEM images in Fig. S2, it can be seen that the GQDs (2.0) solution has the largest particle size and the widest size distribution and the GQDs (4.0) solution has the smallest particle size and the narrowest size distribution. The small particle size and the narrow size distribution will make the attraction among the QD particles become smaller and the agglomeration phenomenon among the QDs becomes less obvious, resulting in a slower decrease in the absorbance of the GQDs solution. On the contrary, the larger the particle size and the wider the size distribution will cause the attraction among the QD particles to become large and the agglomeration phenomenon become more intense, resulting in a faster decrease in the absorbance of the GQDs solution. Therefore, the prepared GQDs (4.0) solution has the best stability and the GQDs (2.0) solution has the worst stability at different venturi inlet pressures. Further, it can also be seen from the figure that after 5 weeks, the decreases in absorbance of GQDs (2.0), GQDs (3.0) and GQDs (4.0) solutions are 24.86 %, 7.73 % and 5.73 %, respectively. This demonstrates that the stability of GQDs prepared by HC technique is proportional to the inlet pressure of the venturi. Within a certain pressure range, the GQDs solution with good stability can be obtained by increasing the inlet pressure of the venturi. In addition, a stability of the GQDs solution prepared by HC technique may decrease after prolonged storage. At this time, if the GQDs solution can be placed in the HC equipment for a short period of HC cycling operation, the good dispersion and high concentration of the GQDs solution can be recovered quickly. This operation allows the prepared GQDs solution to be in the best state at all time, which is very beneficial for the preparation and commercialization of GQDs on a certain scale.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of fluid temperature on the composition, particle size, optical properties and stability of GQDs
Effect of fluid temperature on the composition and particle size of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD technique was used to investigate the effect of different fluid temperatures (40 °C, 50 °C and 60 °C) on the crystal structure of the GQDs prepared at 10 h HC cycle time and 3.0 bar venturi inlet pressure. Fig. S6 shows the XRD patterns of GQDs prepared at different fluid temperatures and a standard PDF card (PDF#26-1076) for carbon. From the figure, it can be clearly seen that the XRD patterns of the GQDs samples all contain four diffraction peaks. The peaks at 2θ values of 26.56°, 42.36°, 44.57° and 54.69° correspond to the (002), (010), (011), and (004) crystal planes of the graphite, respectively. This indicates that the diffraction peak positions of the GQDs samples obtained at different fluid temperatures are consistent with those of graphite. Furthermore, it can also be seen from the figure that the diffraction peak height order of the obtained GQDs samples is 40 °C > 50 °C > 60 °C and the diffraction peak width (0.2426 (40 °C), 0.3021 (50 °C) and 0.3145 (60 °C)) order is 40 °C < 50 °C < 60 °C at different venturi inlet pressures. This may be due to the fact that the particle size of the prepared GQDs decreases with the increase of fluid temperature. It can be found that the diffraction peak height of the GQDs prepared at 60 °C fluid temperature is the lowest and the diffraction peak width is the widest. Since the particle size of the QDs is inversely proportional to the half-peak width of the diffraction peak, the GQDs obtained at 60 °C fluid temperature shows the smallest particle size. In addition, the cavitation number C v can be used to further explain this phenomenon. The C v value can be used to represent the strength of the HC effect to some extent. When the C v value is between 0.1 and 1.0, a small C v value represents a strong HC effect. Formulas (2) and (3) can both be used to calculate the cavitation number C v value. From formula (3), it can be seen that the cavitation number C v value is related to the saturation vapor pressure of the solution. As the saturation vapor pressure of the solution increases, the C v value will become small. For an aqueous solution, the saturated vapor pressure of water gradually increases as the temperature of the water increases. Therefore, it can be assumed that the calculated C v value decreases as the fluid temperature increases. Moreover, the C v values of the GQDs obtained at different fluid temperatures by using formula (2) are 0.786 (40 °C), 0.448 (50 °C) and 0.315 (60 °C), respectively. It can be seen that the obtained C v value decreases as the fluid temperature increases, which is consistent with the conclusion drawn from formula (3). It can be seen that the calculated C v value is the smallest and the produced HC effect is the strongest at 60 °C fluid temperature, which will lead to the smallest particle size of the obtained GQDs. In summary, increasing the fluid temperature is conducive to preparing GQDs with small particle size.

In 2022, Sun et al. investigated the HC degradation of Auramine O dye and heat generation by using orifice plate. It was found that a strong HC effect can be obtained by increasing the fluid temperature in the range of 30 °C–60 °C fluid temperature [64]. In the same year, Wang et al. studied the HC degradation of Rhodamine B and heat generation under the thermal assistance condition. It was also found that a strong HC effect can be achieved by preheating the fluid [65]. The TEM technology was utilized to more clearly and accurately observe the particle size and size distribution of the GQDs prepared at different fluid temperatures (40 °C, 50 °C and 60 °C). Fig. S7(a–c) shows the TEM images of the GQDs obtained at different fluid temperatures and the insets in the upper right corner of these three TEM images are the particle size distribution maps (the number of GQDs used for particle size analysis is 100). Some GQDs particles are marked with yellow circles in the figure. It is evident in Fig. S7(a–c) that the GQDs obtained at different fluid temperatures (40 °C, 50 °C and 60 °C) all have spherical morphology and good dispersity. Specifically, it can be seen from the size distribution maps that the average sizes of the GQDs produced at different fluid temperatures are 2.29 ± 0.03 nm (40 °C), 1.95 ± 0.03 nm (50 °C) and 1.91 ± 0.01 nm (60 °C), respectively, and the size distribution ranges of the attained GQDs are 1.00–3.50 nm (40 °C), 1.00–3.00 nm (50 °C) and 1.25–2.50 nm (60 °C), respectively. It can be found that the GQDs obtained at 40 °C fluid temperature have the largest particle size and the widest size distribution and the GQDs obtained at 60 °C fluid temperature have the smallest particle size and the narrowest size distribution. Compared with other reported studies, it can be seen that the particle size of GQDs prepared by HC technology is smaller and the size distribution is more uniform [52–54]. In addition, the above-mentioned phenomenon is consistent with that observed in the XRD pattern, indicating that the particle size of GQDs decreases as the fluid temperature increases. However, it can also be observed from the size distribution maps that the particle size of GQDs obtained at 60 °C fluid temperature is only slightly decreased compared to that of GQDs obtained at 50 °C fluid temperature. Although the further increase of fluid temperature is beneficial for the preparation of GQDs with small particle size, the particle size reduction magnitude of the obtained GQDs is small. Considering the safety of operation, the heat resistance and service life of the PPR pipe and the other adverse effects (for example, the decrease in service life of the pump, the damage of the flow meters and pressure gauges and the increase in maintenance cost) under constant high temperature for the HC equipment during the preparation process of GQDs, 50 °C fluid temperature is used in the actual production process to carry out the GQDs preparation on a certain scale. In conclusion, the GQDs with small particle size can be obtained by adjusting the fluid temperature in a certain temperature range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of fluid temperature on optical properties of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to analyze the optical properties of the GQDs prepared at different fluid temperatures (40 °C, 50 °C and 60 °C). Fig. S8 shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of GQDs solutions prepared at different fluid temperatures. In the figure, it can be found that there are two obvious absorption peaks (at 230 nm and 275 nm) for all GQDs solutions. Specifically, the typical absorption peak at 230 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 structural domain and the absorption peak at 275 nm may be ascribed to the n-π* transition between the oxygen-containing group and the sp2 structural domain. In addition, the UV–vis spectra can be used to analyze concentration changes of the samples. It can be seen that the peak intensity orders of the absorption peaks at 230 nm and 275 nm wavelengths of the GQDs solutions obtained at different fluid temperatures all are 40 °C < 50 °C < 60 °C. It can be found that the concentration of GQDs solution increases with rising fluid temperature, indicating that increasing the fluid temperatures is beneficial for the preparation of high concentration of GQDs solution. The concentrations of GQDs solutions are determined by ICP technique and are 0.76 g/L (40 °C), 1.18 g/L (50 °C) and 1.69 g/L (60 °C), respectively. Further, it can also be seen that the width orders of the absorption peaks all are 40 °C > 50 °C > 60 °C. The width variation of the absorption peaks may be related to the size distribution of the prepared GQDs. It can be found that the absorption peak width of GQDs solution obtained at 40 °C fluid temperature is the largest and that of GQDs obtained at 60 °C fluid temperature is the smallest, indicating that increasing the fluid temperature is advantageous for the preparation of size-uniform GQDs. In addition, it can be found that the concentration and absorbance of the GQDs solution obtained at 60 °C fluid temperature are slightly high compared to those of GQDs obtained at 50 °C fluid temperature. Although further increasing the fluid temperature is favorable for preparing high concentration of GQDs on the basis of 50 °C fluid temperature, the concentration increase magnitude of the obtained GQDs is small. This is consistent with the conclusions obtained from TEM image analysis. In conclusion, a higher concentration and particle size uniform of GQDs can be achieved by appropriately increasing fluid temperature.

The PL spectra of the obtained GQDs were investigated to further explore the optical properties of GQDs prepared at different fluid temperatures (40 °C, 50 °C and 60 °C). The quartz cuvette with four optical paths was utilized in fluorescence measurements. Fig. S9(a–c) shows the PL spectra of GQDs prepared at different fluid temperatures under the excitation of different wavelengths, respectively. Fig. S9(d) shows the emission spectra of GQDs sample solutions at 225 nm excitation wavelength and the inset in the upper right corner of the figure is a photograph of the GQDs solutions obtained at different fluid temperatures under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp. As can be seen from Fig. S9(a–c), when the excitation wavelength is lower than 225 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs prepared using HC technique gradually increases with the increase of excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity reaches the highest value at the 225 nm excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength is >225 nm, the fluorescence intensity decreases with the increase of the excitation wavelength. However, it can be found that the peak position (363 nm) of the emission spectra shows no significant red-shift or blue-shift phenomenon at different excitation wavelengths. This phenomenon belongs to the typical excitation-independent PL behavior, which may be attributed to the homogeneous microstructure, low defect and similar chemical state of the GQDs prepared using the HC technique. It can be also seen from the PL spectra that the strongest emission of the prepared GQDs occurs at 225 nm with a Stokes shift of 138 nm.

In Fig. S9(d), it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the prepared GQDs increases as the fluid temperature increases at the 225 nm excitation wavelength. This may be caused by the higher concentration and smaller particle size of the obtained GQDs when the fluid temperature increases. It can also be seen from the upper right inset that the luminescence of the obtained GQDs solution under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light becomes brighter when the fluid temperature increases. Further, the fluorescence QY of the GQDs solution is calculated to investigate the fluorescence properties of the GQDs solution. The calculated QYs are 15.26 % (40 °C), 24.24 % (50 °C) and 33.54 % (60 °C), respectively. It can be seen that the fluorescence QY of the obtained GQDs solution becomes large when the fluid temperature increases, which may be related to the concentration of the GQDs solution and the particle size of GQDs. In summary, it can be found that appropriately elevating the fluid temperature is beneficial for the preparation of GQDs with excellent optical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of fluid temperature on the stability of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis spectra of GQDs samples prepared at different fluid temperatures (40 °C, 50 °C and 60 °C) were periodically tested to assess the stability of the obtained GQDs solutions. Fig. S10 shows the percentage decrease in absorbance of GQDs solutions obtained at different fluid temperatures. It can be seen from the figure that the decreased degree in absorbance of the GQDs (GQDs (60)) solution is smallest and that of GQDs (GQDs (40)) solution is the largest. The decreased degree of absorbance of GQDs (GQDs (50)) solution is between those of GQDs (40) and GQDs (60) solutions. This may be related to the particle size and size distribution of GQDs prepared by using HC technique. From the analysis of the TEM images in Fig. S7, it can be seen that the GQDs (40) solution has the largest particle size and the widest size distribution and the GQDs (60) solution has the smallest particle size and the narrowest size distribution. When the particle size is small and the distribution is narrow, the attraction among the QDs is small and the agglomeration phenomenon is not obvious, resulting in a small absorbance decrease of the GQDs solution. On the contrary, when the particle size is large and the distribution is wide, the attraction among the QDs is large and the agglomeration phenomenon is strong, which will lead to a significant absorbance decrease of the GQDs solution. Therefore, the prepared GQDs (60) solution has the best stability and the GQDs (40) solution has the worst stability. Further, it can also be seen from the figure that after 5 weeks, the absorbance decreases of GQDs (40), GQDs (50) and GQDs (60) solutions are 43.81 %, 7.73 % and 6.15 %, respectively. This indicates that the stability of GQDs prepared by using HC technique is positively correlated with the fluid temperature to some extent. Within a certain fluid temperature range, the GQDs solution with good stability can be attained by appropriately increasing the fluid temperature. In addition, the stability of the GQDs solution prepared by HC technique may decrease after prolonged storage. At this time, if the GQDs solution can be placed in the HC equipment for a short period of HC cycling operation, the good dispersion and high concentration of the GQDs solution can be recovered quickly. This operation allows the prepared GQDs solution to be in the best state at all time, which is very advantageous for the preparation, prolonged storage and commercialization of GQDs on a certain scale.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of GQDs preparation
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, first, expanded graphite powder was irradiated with a microwave oven. The expansion phenomenon of expandable graphite powder occurs, which leads to the volume of the expandable graphite powder increasing significantly. Then, the expanded graphite powder is placed in HC equipment to prepare GQDs. Fig. 11(a) shows a schematic diagram of the whole preparation process of GQDs. Fig. 11(b and c) are the principal diagrams of the preparation of GQDs by using HC technique. The whole preparation process of GQDs is divided into two parts (pretreatment and HC treatment). The possible mechanism of the pretreatment operation is as follows. The expandable graphite is stacked with millions of graphene layers. A variety of means (for example, the strong oxidizing ability of acids and the penetrating ability of supercritical fluids) can be used to separate these graphene layers. In this work, microwave technology is used to break up these stacked graphene layers. Microwave irradiation can weaken/disrupt the van der Waals interlayer bonds among different graphene layers. When the van der Waals interlayer bonds among the different graphene layers of the expandable graphite powder is weakened or even broken, the bonding among these graphene layers will be broken and the different graphene layers will separate, resulting in the expansion of the expandable graphite powder. The possible mechanism of HC treatment is as follows. The HC treatment mainly utilizes the HC effect produced in the HC device. According to Bernoulli's principle, when fluid flows into a throttling device (for example, venturi), the flow velocity of the fluid increases, which causes a rapid decrease of the fluid internal pressure (as shown in Fig. 11(b)). When the fluid internal pressure drops below the saturated vapor pressure of the fluid, some of the fluid will vaporize and the vaporized fluid can form cavities. With the further decrease of pressure, the cavities will gradually become large, forming unstable cavitation bubbles with large amounts of energy. As the fluid flows through the throttling device, the pressure of the fluid begins to recover, and the unstable cavitation bubbles will be compressed by constant recovery pressure, which will eventually cause the fracture and collapse of the cavitation bubbles. The fracture and collapse of cavitation bubbles can create high temperature (5000–10,000 K) and high pressure (500–1000 atm) and release a large quantity of energy, accompanied by the generation of violent shock waves, shear force and high-speed (100 m/s) jet flow. Specifically, the chemical bonds inside graphene vibrate at high temperature (5000–10,000 K). The breakage of chemical bonds may occur under prolonged high temperature. The graphene layers of the expanded graphite powder are squeezed under high pressure (500–1000 atm), which leads to further disruption of the remaining van der Waals interlayer bonds among the graphene layers. The expanded graphite powder is broken into graphene sheets. Violent shock waves and high-speed jet flow can directly impact the surface of these graphene sheets. The graphene sheets are broken into smaller graphene fragments under the repeated action of the shock wave and jet flow. The shear force can make the graphene fragments collide and rub with each other many times, which makes the graphene fragments further become smaller and the surface of the graphene fragments becomes smooth. After a long period of HC cycle operation, graphene fragments are broken into GQDs and the surface morphology of the GQDs is collided and rubbed into a smooth spherical shape.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The advantages and novelty of the GQDs prepared by HC method
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the preparation method (HC) of GQDs with simple operation, low cost, high safety and potential for scaled production is proposed. The small particle size, uniform size distribution, strong blue luminescence ability, low defects, good water solubility and high quantum yield of GQDs can be obtained by HC method. And no impurities were introduced and concentrated sulfuric acid and nitric acid were not used during the entire preparation process. The abundant and inexpensive expandable graphite powder was chosen as the starting raw material to prepare GQDs. In the purification of the prepared sample solution, only simple filtration is required to obtain the GQDs solution. The unreacted and insufficiently exfoliated graphite flakes can be recovered for recycling in theory. So, the maximum theoretical yield of GQDs prepared by using HC technique is close to 100 %. In addition, the GQDs obtained in this work are water-soluble and do not contain other impurities. Compared with the GQDs generated by adding organic solvents or strong acids, the properties of the GQDs prepared in this work will not be affected by the added substances during the preparation process and its luminescence is much purer and the quality of the finished product is higher. Finally, HC technology has the advantages of inexpensive equipment, simple operation, low running cost and high processing capacity, which is very beneficial to the production and commercialization of GQDs on a certain scale in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the preparation of GQDs by using HC technique is investigated. The results showed that the GQDs obtained under the conditions of 14 h HC cycle time, 3.0 bar venturi inlet pressure and 50 °C fluid temperature have the smallest particle size of 1.77 ± 0.03 nm, the narrowest size distribution range of 1.25–2.25 nm and the highest fluorescence quantum yield of 36.77 %. Considering the actual production process, graphene sheets that are not completely exfoliated into GQDs can be recycled. It is believed that the theoretical maximum yield of GQDs prepared by using HC technique could be close to 100 % by optimizing the experimental setup parameters, improving the experimental conditions and recycling the incompletely exfoliated graphene sheets. In addition, a possible mechanism for the preparation of GQDs by using HC technique is also proposed. In conclusion, a green, simple operation and low - cost method for the production of byproduct-free GQDs on a certain scale has been developed successfully. The GQDs prepared by using HC technique have the advantages of small particle size, uniform size distribution, low defects, good water solubility, high stability and high QY. Moreover, the presence of single-layer GQDs in the GQDs solution prepared by using HC technique can be seen from atomic force microscopy (AFM) images. Therefore, the preparation of single-layer GQDs can be achieved by adjusting the equipment parameters and experimental conditions. It is hoped that the present work can provide a possibility for the preparation of few-layer GQDs on a certain scale.
